input.query_title: Ising Model on Networks with an Arbitrary Distribution of Connections
input.query_abstract: We find the exact critical temperature $T_c$ of the nearest-neighbor ferromagnetic Ising model on an `equilibrium' random graph with an arbitrary degree distribution $P(k)$. We observe an anomalous behavior of the magnetization, magnetic susceptibility and specific heat, when $P(k)$ is fat-tailed, or, loosely speaking, when the fourth moment of the distribution diverges in infinite networks. When the second moment becomes divergent, $T_c$ approaches infinity, the phase transition is of infinite order, and size effect is anomalously strong.
input.hyp_generic_object: binary adoption states of individuals embedded in a social-influence network with an arbitrary distribution of ties
input.hyp_abstract: We obtain the exact tipping point for the collective adoption of a behavior diffusing through a social-influence network in which the number of ties per individual follows an arbitrary distribution P(k). Each actor is represented as a binary unit — adopter or non-adopter — whose propensity to adopt rises with the share of adopting neighbors, and we resolve the resulting peer-influence dynamics through a self-consistent equation for the aggregate adoption level. The critical strength of interpersonal influence at which a population shifts from near-universal non-adoption to widespread adoption is determined by the tie distribution, depending on the ratio between the average number of ties and the mean squared number of ties. When ties are heavy-tailed — a population dominated by a few extremely well-connected influentials — the aggregate adoption level, its responsiveness to marginal changes in influence, and the dispersion of eventual reach all display anomalous behavior once the second moment of the tie distribution diverges. In this regime the tipping point recedes toward zero, so even weak social pressure can ignite society-wide diffusion; the passage from minority to majority adoption ceases to be abrupt and instead unfolds with an anomalously gradual onset, and the outcome stays strongly sensitive to the size of the community. We draw out implications for seeding strategies in viral marketing and collective-action campaigns, where influence concentrated on the most connected members disproportionately governs whether a cascade takes off.
input.candidate_title: Social consensus through the influence of committed minorities
input.candidate_abstract: We show how the prevailing majority opinion in a population can be rapidly reversed by a small fraction p of randomly distributed committed agents who consistently proselytize the opposing opinion and are immune to influence. Specifically, we show that when the committed fraction grows beyond a critical value p_c \approx 10%, there is a dramatic decrease in the time, T_c, taken for the entire population to adopt the committed opinion. In particular, for complete graphs we show that when p < p_c, T_c \sim \exp(α(p)N), while for p > p_c, T_c \sim \ln N. We conclude with simulation results for Erdős-Rényi random graphs and scale-free networks which show qualitatively similar behavior.
